questions.text: Which OSI layer is responsible for finding the best path (routing) for data packets?
questions.explanation:
**Layer 3 (Network)** is responsible for logical addressing and routing.

It decides the best path for a packet to travel from source to destination — across multiple networks.

- Layer 2 handles MAC addresses within the same network
- Layer 1 handles raw bit transmission
- Layer 4 handles end-to-end communication between applications

questions.text: At which layer of the OSI model do MAC addresses operate?
questions.explanation:
**MAC addresses** operate at Layer 2 (Data Link).

MAC stands for Media Access Control. It is a hardware address burned into every network card.

- Layer 2 uses MAC addresses to deliver frames within the same local network
- Layer 3 uses IP addresses to route packets between different networks

questions.text: Which tool is often described as the "Microscope" of the network to analyze packets?
questions.explanation:
**Wireshark** is a packet analyzer — it captures and displays every packet on the network in detail.

- Ping tests if a host is reachable
- Traceroute shows the path a packet takes hop by hop
- Ipcalc calculates IP subnet information

Wireshark lets you inspect headers at every OSI layer, like a microscope for network traffic.

questions.text: When receiving data, the process of removing headers layer by layer is called:
questions.explanation:
**Decapsulation** is the reverse of encapsulation.

When sending data, each layer adds a header → **encapsulation**.
When receiving data, each layer removes its header → **decapsulation**.

Example (receiving):
- Layer 1 receives bits
- Layer 2 removes the Ethernet frame header
- Layer 3 removes the IP header
- Layer 4 removes the TCP/UDP header
- The application gets the original data

questions.text: Which protocol is used to establish a reliable connection at Layer 4?
questions.explanation:
**TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol) operates at Layer 4 and provides a reliable connection.

TCP guarantees:
- Delivery of all packets
- Correct order of packets
- Error detection via acknowledgments (ACK)

UDP is also Layer 4 but is unreliable (no guarantees). IP is Layer 3. HTTP is Layer 7.

questions.text: What is the primary role of the Physical Layer (Layer 1)?

options.A: Transmitting electrical, optical, or radio signals (Bits)
options.C: Determining the best path for a packet

questions.explanation:
**Layer 1 (Physical)** deals with the raw transmission of bits over a physical medium.

This includes:
- Electrical signals (copper cable)
- Light pulses (fiber optic)
- Radio waves (Wi-Fi)

Layer 1 does not understand addresses or logic — it just transmits 0s and 1s.

questions.text: Which type of address is unique to each network card and is often called a "hardware address"?
questions.explanation:
A **MAC address** is a unique identifier assigned to a network interface card (NIC) by the manufacturer.

- It is 48 bits long, written as `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`
- It is permanent (burned into hardware)
- It operates at Layer 2

IP addresses are logical and can change. MAC addresses are physical and unique to the device.

questions.text: Which network device works primarily at Layer 2 and makes decisions based on MAC addresses?
questions.explanation:
A **Switch** operates at Layer 2 and forwards frames based on MAC addresses.

- It maintains a MAC address table to know which device is on which port
- It only sends frames to the correct destination port (not to everyone)

A Hub (Layer 1) sends data to all ports. A Router (Layer 3) uses IP addresses. A Repeater just amplifies the signal.

questions.text: What is a "collision domain"?

options.A: A group of devices that receive broadcast messages
options.B: An area of the network where two messages sent simultaneously can collide
options.C: The IP address range of a local network

questions.explanation:
A **collision domain** is a network segment where two devices can transmit at the same time and their signals can interfere (collide).

- A Hub creates one big collision domain for all connected devices
- A Switch separates each port into its own collision domain — eliminating collisions

Note: A **broadcast domain** (option A) is different — it is the area that receives broadcast messages.

questions.text: Which protocol resolves a MAC address from a known IP address?
questions.explanation:
**ARP** (Address Resolution Protocol) finds the MAC address when you already know the IP address.

How it works:
1. Device A wants to send data to IP `192.168.1.5`
2. A broadcasts: "Who has `192.168.1.5`?"
3. The device with that IP replies with its MAC address

- DNS resolves domain names to IPs
- DHCP assigns IPs automatically
- ICMP is used for diagnostic messages (like Ping)

questions.text: What is the universal MAC broadcast address?
questions.explanation:
**FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF** is the Layer 2 broadcast MAC address.

A frame sent to this address is delivered to **all devices** on the local network segment.

- `00:00:00:00:00:00` is not a valid broadcast address
- `127.0.0.1` is the IP loopback address (Layer 3)
- `255.255.255.255` is the IP broadcast address (Layer 3)

questions.text: Which device is commonly used to connect multiple devices within the same local network (LAN)?
questions.explanation:
A **Switch** is the standard device for connecting multiple devices in a LAN.

- It forwards data only to the correct destination (using MAC addresses)
- It supports full-duplex communication on each port

A Router connects different networks. A Modem connects to the internet provider. A Firewall controls traffic but does not primarily connect devices.

questions.text: Which newer version of the IP protocol was created because the world ran out of IPv4 addresses?
questions.explanation:
**IPv6** was created to solve the IPv4 address exhaustion problem.

- IPv4 uses 32 bits → ~4.3 billion addresses (not enough)
- IPv6 uses 128 bits → ~340 undecillion addresses

IPv6 addresses look like: `2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334`

Note: IPv5 was an experimental protocol, never widely deployed. IPv3 and IPv10 do not exist.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a Router in a network?

options.A: To connect different networks together and direct traffic
options.B: To connect devices within the same office
options.C: To provide electricity to connected devices
options.D: To store and play music files

questions.explanation:
A **Router** operates at Layer 3 and connects multiple networks together.

It:
- Reads IP addresses in packets
- Chooses the best path using its routing table
- Forwards packets toward their destination network

A Switch connects devices within the same network. A Router connects networks to each other (e.g., your home network to the internet).

questions.text: Which of these is a private IP address (used only inside a local network)?
questions.explanation:
**192.168.1.50** is a private IP address — it can only be used inside a local network.

Private IP ranges (RFC 1918):
- `10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255`
- `172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255`
- `192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255`

The others are public:
- `8.8.8.8` → Google DNS
- `1.1.1.1` → Cloudflare DNS
- `142.250.0.1` → Google server

questions.text: What does the /24 subnet mask represent in decimal notation?
questions.explanation:
**/24** means the first 24 bits are the network part → `255.255.255.0`.

CIDR to decimal:
- `/8` → `255.0.0.0`
- `/16` → `255.255.0.0`
- `/24` → `255.255.255.0`
- `/30` → `255.255.255.252`

A /24 network contains 256 addresses (254 usable hosts).

questions.text: Which address is the default Loopback address?
questions.explanation:
**127.0.0.1** is the loopback address — also called `localhost`.

When you send data to `127.0.0.1`, it stays on your own machine (it loops back). No network traffic is actually sent.

- `0.0.0.0` means "all interfaces" or "no specific address"
- `169.254.x.x` is an APIPA address (assigned when DHCP fails)
- `192.168.1.1` is typically a router's local IP

questions.text: What is the role of a "Default Gateway"?

options.A: It is the router address used to send data outside the local network
options.B: It is the DNS server address
options.C: It is the main switch's MAC address
options.D: It is the IP address of the computer itself

questions.explanation:
The **Default Gateway** is the IP address of the router on your local network.

When your computer wants to reach a destination outside the local network:
1. It checks: is the destination in my subnet?
2. If no → it sends the packet to the Default Gateway
3. The router then forwards the packet toward the internet

Without a default gateway, your machine cannot communicate outside its own network.

questions.text: If a router has multiple routes to a destination, which one will it prioritize?

options.B: The shortest route in terms of physical distance
options.D: The route with the most specific subnet mask (longest prefix)

questions.explanation:
Routers use the **longest prefix match** rule: the more specific route wins.

Example — destination IP: `192.168.1.25`:
- Route A: `192.168.0.0/16` → matches
- Route B: `192.168.1.0/24` → also matches, but more specific

The router picks Route B (`/24`) because it has a longer prefix.

The default route `0.0.0.0/0` has the shortest prefix (matches everything) so it is always the last resort.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the TTL (Time To Live) field in an IP packet?

options.B: To prevent a packet from looping infinitely on the network
options.C: To indicate the packet creation time

questions.explanation:
**TTL (Time To Live)** is a counter in the IP header that prevents packets from looping forever.

How it works:
- The sender sets a TTL value (e.g., 64)
- Each router that forwards the packet decrements TTL by 1
- When TTL reaches 0, the router drops the packet and sends an ICMP error back

This protects the network from routing loops that could fill all bandwidth.

questions.text: Which tool deliberately uses TTL to identify each router on the path to a target?
questions.explanation:
**Traceroute** (or `tracert` on Windows) uses TTL to discover each hop on the path.

How it works:
1. Send a packet with TTL=1 → first router drops it and replies (identified!)
2. Send with TTL=2 → second router replies (identified!)
3. Repeat until destination is reached

Ping only tests reachability. Ipconfig shows your own network configuration. `arp -a` shows the ARP cache.

questions.text: Which command is used to test if a remote machine is reachable and responding?
questions.explanation:
**Ping** sends ICMP Echo Request packets to a target and waits for Echo Reply packets.

It tells you:
- Is the host alive and reachable?
- How long does the round-trip take (latency in ms)?
- Are any packets lost?

`ipconfig` shows your own network configuration — it does not test remote machines.

questions.text: What problem does the Transport Layer (Layer 4) solve compared to Layer 3?

options.A: Routing data between two remote machines
options.C: Managing collisions on the Ethernet cable
options.D: Identifying which specific application on the machine should receive the data

questions.explanation:
Layer 3 delivers a packet to the right **machine** (using IP address).

But a machine runs many applications at once — browser, email, game...

**Layer 4 (Transport)** adds **port numbers** to identify which application should receive the data.

Example:
- IP `192.168.1.5` → the right machine (Layer 3)
- Port `443` → the web browser on that machine (Layer 4)

questions.text: What is a "Socket"?

options.B: The combination of an IP address, a port number, and a protocol
options.D: A type of fiber optic cable

questions.explanation:
A **Socket** is the combination of an IP address + port number + protocol.

Example: `TCP 192.168.1.5:443`

A socket uniquely identifies one end of a network connection:
- IP address → which machine
- Port number → which application
- Protocol → TCP or UDP

Two sockets (client + server) form a complete connection.

questions.text: What is the range of "Well-known ports"?
questions.explanation:
**Well-known ports** are in the range **0–1023**. They are reserved for standard protocols.

Examples:
- Port 80 → HTTP
- Port 443 → HTTPS
- Port 22 → SSH
- Port 53 → DNS

Port ranges:
- 0–1023 → Well-known (system)
- 1024–49151 → Registered
- 49152–65535 → Dynamic / ephemeral (used by clients)

questions.text: Which characteristic belongs to the UDP protocol?

options.A: Fast, connectionless, and with low overhead
options.D: Reorders packets if they arrive out of sequence

questions.explanation:
**UDP** (User Datagram Protocol) is fast and lightweight — but unreliable.

UDP characteristics:
- No connection setup (connectionless)
- No acknowledgments → no guaranteed delivery
- No reordering of packets
- Very low overhead → fast

TCP has the 3-way handshake, guaranteed delivery, and reordering. UDP trades those guarantees for speed.

questions.text: Which protocol is better suited for live video streaming because it is fast and doesn't wait for lost packets?
questions.explanation:
**UDP** is preferred for live video streaming.

Why:
- Live video cannot wait for retransmission of lost packets (that would cause freezing)
- A slightly degraded frame is better than a delayed one
- UDP's low overhead keeps latency minimal

TCP would pause and retransmit every lost packet — unacceptable for real-time media.

UDP is also used for: online gaming, VoIP, DNS queries.

questions.text: What is the standard port number used for secure web browsing (HTTPS)?
questions.explanation:
**Port 443** is the standard port for **HTTPS** (HTTP over TLS).

Key port numbers to remember:
- 80 → HTTP (unencrypted web)
- 443 → HTTPS (encrypted web)
- 21 → FTP (file transfer)
- 53 → DNS (domain name resolution)
- 22 → SSH (secure remote access)

questions.text: In TCP, what is the purpose of an "Acknowledgment" (ACK) message?

options.A: To encrypt the data being sent
options.B: To ask the router for a new IP address
options.C: To confirm that a segment has been successfully received
options.D: To measure the length of the cable

questions.explanation:
In TCP, an **ACK (Acknowledgment)** confirms that a segment was received successfully.

How TCP reliability works:
1. Sender sends segment 1
2. Receiver gets it → sends ACK
3. Sender sends segment 2...
4. If no ACK is received within a timeout → sender retransmits

This is what makes TCP **reliable** — every segment must be acknowledged, or it is sent again.

questions.text: Which port number is used by default by the HTTP protocol?
questions.explanation:
**Port 80** is the default port for **HTTP** (HyperText Transfer Protocol).

When you type `http://example.com` in a browser, it automatically connects to port 80.

- Port 443 is for HTTPS (encrypted)
- Port 53 is for DNS
- Port 22 is for SSH

If a web server runs on a non-standard port, you must specify it in the URL: `http://example.com:8080`
